title: First Day at Microsoft Full-Time!
content: Officially started as a full-time TPM at Microsoft today. The journey from intern to full-time has been surreal. Excited to dive into Windows Engineering Systems and AI Developer Productivity!
detail:
Today marks the beginning of an incredible new chapter. Walking into Building 25 in Redmond as a full-time employee felt completely different from my intern days last summer. The badge now says 'Microsoft' without an expiration date, and that hit differently.

I'll be working on Windows Engineering Systems within the AI Developer Productivity space — essentially helping developers across Microsoft work more efficiently using AI-powered tools. It's the perfect intersection of program management, technology, and enabling others that I've always been drawn to.

Looking back, the path here wasn't linear. From research at Oregon State, to internships at Lockheed Martin, HP, and Microsoft, each step taught me something different. But they all pointed here. Ready to make an impact.

title: Exploring the PNW
content: Spent the weekend hiking in the Cascades. The fall foliage is starting to turn and the views from the summit were absolutely breathtaking. Washington state really is something special.
detail:
Washington state continues to blow my mind. This weekend I tackled a trail in the North Cascades and the fall colors were just starting to appear — patches of gold and orange against deep green evergreens with snow-capped peaks in the distance.

Growing up across Puerto Rico, Michigan, D.C., Texas, Virginia, and Oregon, I've seen a lot of different landscapes. But the PNW has this unique combination of mountains, water, and forests that makes every hike feel like stepping into a painting.

The summit views were absolutely worth the climb. Already planning the next one — maybe Rainier before the snow gets too heavy.

title: Sustainability & Upcycling Projects
content: Been getting more into upcycling furniture and DIY sustainability projects. Turned an old pallet into a bookshelf this weekend. Carrying forward the spirit of the intern sustainability club!
detail:
During my Microsoft internship in summer 2024, I co-founded an Intern Co-Creation club centered around sustainability and upcycling. That experience sparked something. Now I'm carrying that energy forward into my personal life.

This weekend's project: turning a discarded shipping pallet into a rustic bookshelf. Sanded it down, treated the wood, added some brackets, and it turned out way better than expected. There's something deeply satisfying about giving new life to something that would've ended up in a landfill.

Next on the list: a coffee table from reclaimed wood and some planters from old cans. The apartment is slowly becoming a gallery of repurposed things.

title: Cooking Adventures: Puerto Rican Recipes
content: Tried recreating my family's mofongo recipe from scratch. Not quite abuela-level yet, but getting closer. Growing up in Puerto Rico gave me a deep appreciation for good food.
detail:
Growing up in Puerto Rico left me with a deep love for food that goes beyond just eating — it's about community, family, and tradition. This weekend I attempted my family's mofongo recipe from scratch: fried green plantains, garlic, pork cracklings (chicharrón), all mashed together in a pilón.

The result? Honestly, about 7/10. The garlic ratio was perfect but my plantains could've been crispier before mashing. Still, the smell of sofrito filling the apartment made it feel like home.

Cooking Puerto Rican food in Seattle is its own adventure — finding good plantains and proper seasonings takes some hunting. But every time I make something from the island, it connects me back to where I grew up and the people who shaped me.

title: Reflecting on SIGCSE 2023
content: Looking back at presenting at SIGCSE in Toronto — still one of my proudest moments. The work on making CS more equitable through game-oriented curricula continues to inspire what I do.
detail:
Two years later and presenting at SIGCSE 2023 in Toronto still stands as one of the highlights of my academic career. Standing in front of researchers and educators from around the world, presenting the data analysis section of our paper 'Putting Computing on the Table' — it was nerve-wracking and exhilarating.

The conference itself was eye-opening. SIGCSE is CORE A-ranked with a ~30% acceptance rate, so just having our work accepted was a milestone. But the conversations afterwards — with people who cared about making CS accessible, who were experimenting with similar approaches, who saw the value in game-based learning — those were the real treasure.

This work under Dr. Parham-Mocello showed me that research isn't just about publications. It's about impact. Every kid who engages with CS through a game instead of being intimidated by a blank IDE screen — that's what it's all about.

title: Weekend Board Games
content: Hosted a board game night with friends. There's something special about physical games and computational thinking coming together — guess my research background is showing!
detail:
Hosted another board game night and it's quickly becoming a regular thing. This time we played Catan, Codenames, and a new one I picked up called Wingspan. The table was full, the snacks were flowing, and the competitive spirit was very much alive.

There's a beautiful irony in the fact that my CS education research was literally about using physical games to teach computer science — and here I am, still obsessed with board games outside of work. But honestly, the research gave me a deeper appreciation for game design: the rule systems, the decision trees, the way good games scaffold complexity.

Board game nights are also just good for the soul. In a world of screens and Slack messages, sitting around a table with friends, laughing over bad dice rolls and clever strategies — it's the perfect reset.

title: Seattle Coffee Tour
content: Trying to hit every independent coffee shop in Capitol Hill. Current favorite: a tiny spot with incredible pour-overs and a warm, cozy atmosphere. The PNW coffee scene is unreal.
detail:
The Seattle coffee scene is genuinely unmatched. I've set myself a goal: visit every independent coffee shop in Capitol Hill. Current count is somewhere around 15, and I'm nowhere near done.

Today's find was a tiny spot tucked behind a bookstore — maybe 8 seats total. Their pour-over was exceptional: complex, fruity, with this beautiful clarity that you only get when someone really cares about the craft. The barista talked me through the bean origin (Ethiopian, naturally processed) and brewing method for a solid 5 minutes.

Favorites so far: the hidden bookstore spot for pour-overs, a cozy corner place on Pike for espresso drinks, and a roastery near the park for their seasonal blends. The PNW coffee culture is less about caffeine and more about community and craft. I love it.

title: Building This Website
content: Finally redesigned my personal website with an organic warm-tone theme. Wanted it to feel like nature meets technology. The diary feature makes it easy to keep adding life updates!
detail:
After months of procrastinating, I finally sat down and redesigned my personal website from scratch. The old version was... functional, but it didn't feel like *me*.

The new design uses a warm organic color palette — terracotta, burnt orange, deep browns, amber — inspired by the Pacific Northwest landscapes I've been exploring. I wanted it to feel like nature meets technology: soft grain textures, blurred organic shapes in the background, smooth scroll-triggered animations.

The diary feature is my favorite part. Each entry lives in a simple JSON file, and the site auto-generates cards with filtering. Now I can add new entries by just editing one file. No CMS, no database, no complexity — just content.

Building this was a nice reminder of why I fell in love with technology in the first place. Sometimes the best projects are the personal ones.

title: 🎉 My 2025 Recap
content: What a year! Graduated from OSU with my M.S. & B.S., presented at IEEE FIE in Nashville, wrapped up 4+ years of CS Ed research, and started full-time at Microsoft. Grateful for everything.
detail:
As 2025 comes to a close, I want to take a moment to recap my year full of some major milestones.

June marked the end of my 5-year stint as a student at Oregon State University, graduating with a Master of Science in Computer Science, a Minor in Mathematics & an 18-credit Graduate Certificate in College & University Teaching ('25); along with an Honors Bachelor of Science in Computer Science with a concentration in Artificial Intelligence (AI) & a Minor in Mathematics, Summa Cum Laude ('24).

During this time, I was extremely fortunate to hold multiple campus-wide jobs; serving as a Graduate Teaching Assistant, Undergraduate Learning Assistant, College of Engineering Ambassador, & Undergraduate Research Assistant. Additionally, I was lucky to be involved as a club officer for Oregon State's Out in Science, Technology, Engineering & Mathematics (oSTEM) chapter. I'm beyond thankful for the friends + community fostered, & am immensely proud to have been a part of the planning committee that sent the chapter to its first national conference back in 2023.

April marked my completion as a two-time graduate of the LEAP program. LEAP provided me with incredible opportunities to foster industry connections & mentors, develop leadership skills aligned with my passions, & strengthen my communication & presentation skills. My LEAP summit project, 'LinkedIn Workshop for Queer Individuals,' which guided queer college students through the process of creating a LinkedIn profile, was selected as one of the top 5 presentations. A very special thank you to Melissa Ward & J Gilley for their dedication to the program.

In November, I travelled to Nashville, TN, to present a synthesized publication of my Master's thesis at IEEE Frontiers in Education Conference (FIE): 'Impacts of Professional Development on Knowledge for Teaching Middle School Computer Science.' This wrapped up one of the greatest highlights of my time at Oregon State: working under Dr. Jennifer Parham-Mocello on Computer Science Education research for over four years, netting two theses & four co-authored publications. What started as curiosity led to an REU & evolved into an incredible mentorship & development of knowledge I'll forever be grateful for.

I'm immensely excited to continue with my next chapter as a Technical Program Manager at Microsoft in Redmond, WA, for 2026 & beyond! I'll eternally be grateful for all the friendships, experiences, & learning that made this next chapter possible ❤️.
